coredata.dc:title: Highly selective determination of dopamine by carbon fiber electrochemical sensor modified with three-dimensional graphene nanosheets and carbon dots

coredata.dc:description: This research introduces an innovative electrochemical sensor architecture for the detection of dopamine (DA), which harnesses a three-dimensional graphene nanosheet network intimately wrapped around activated carbon fibers (ACF), facilitated by an ionic liquid (IL)-mediated electrodeposition approach. By utilizing an ionic liquid ([BMIM][BF4]) as the electrolyte, graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets are tailored with IL molecules through cation–π interactions, efficiently mitigating restacking and fostering the construction of a three-dimensional graphene architecture. Subsequently, carbon dots (CDs) are electrodeposited onto this IL-functionalized graphene/ACF hybrid, contributing abundant carboxyl moieties that electrostatically engage with the amine functionalities of DA. This innovative design not only augments the electrode’s surface area and conductivity but also fosters selective DA binding, thereby intensifying its redox reaction kinetics. The ensuing biosensor exhibits an extensive linear detection range spanning from 0.3 to 8.0 μM for DA, accompanied by an ultra-sensitive limit of detection at 13 nM. Remarkably, its outstanding performance in real-world biological matrices, such as human serum and urine, underscores its immense potential for applications in clinical diagnostics and monitoring therapeutic interventions.

(A) XPS profiles of CDs, GO, and CDs/IL-rGO/ACF. High resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of CDs (B), GO (C), and CDs/IL-rGO/ACF (D). (E) FT-IR spectrum of CDs.

(A) CV curves of ACF (a), CDs/ACF (b), IL-rGO/ACF (c), and CDs/IL-rGO/ACF (d) in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. CV curves of ACF (B), CDs/ACF (C), IL-rGO/ACF (D), and CDs/IL-rGO/ACF (E) in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at different scan rates (25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200, 250, 275, and 300 m V·s−1). Inset: Relationships between Ψ and v −0.5.

(A) CV curves of 5 μM DA at ACF (a), CDs/ACF (b), IL-rGO/ACF (c), and CDs/IL-rGO/ACF (d) in 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 6.0). (B) CV curves of 5 µM DA at CDs/IL-rGO/ACF in 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 6.0) with different scan rates of 10, 20, 50, 80, 100, 120, 150, and 200 mV·s−1. (C) Linear plots of the peak currents versus the scan rate. (D) DPV curves of 5 μM DA at ACF (a), CDs/ACF (b), IL-rGO/ACF (c), and CDs/IL-rGO/ACF (d) in 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 6.0). Inset: DPV peak currents of 5 μM DA at different electrodes in 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 6.0).

Relationships between DPV peak current and the concentration of IL-rGO (A), the deposition potential of IL-rGO (B), the deposition time of IL-rGO (C), the concentration of CDs (D), the deposition potential of CDs (E), the deposition time of CDs (F), and the pH value of 0.1 M PBS buffer (G). (H) Linear relationship between DPV peak potential and pH value of 0.1 M PBS buffer.

(A) DPV curves for different concentration of DA (a to k: 0, 0.3, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 8, 10, 15, 20, and 25 µM). (B) DPV peak current for different concentration of DA (0–25 µM). Linear plots depicting the relationship between DPV peak current and DA concentration.
Selectivity of the CDs/IL-rGO/ACF electrode to different substances.

(A) Relative amperometric response of five CDs/IL-rGO/ACF microelectrodes to successive addition of 1 µM DA in 0.1 M PBS (pH 6.0). (B) Relative amperometric response of same CDs/IL-rGO/ACF microelectrode to successive addition of 1 µM DA in 0.1 M PBS (pH 6.0) for different days. The error bars in each condition represent the standard deviations of three separate measurements.

head.ce:title: Highly selective determination of dopamine by carbon fiber electrochemical sensor modified with three-dimensional graphene nanosheets and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Natural Polymer Chemistry and Physics, Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensor Analysis, College of Chemistry and Materials, Nanning Normal University, Nanning 530001 China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research introduces an innovative electrochemical sensor architecture for the detection of dopamine (DA), which harnesses a three-dimensional graphene nanosheet network intimately wrapped around activated carbon fibers (ACF), facilitated by an ionic liquid (IL)-mediated electrodeposition approach. By utilizing an ionic liquid ([BMIM][BF4]) as the electrolyte, graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets are tailored with IL molecules through cation–π interactions, efficiently mitigating restacking and fostering the construction of a three-dimensional graphene architecture. Subsequently, carbon dots (CDs) are electrodeposited onto this IL-functionalized graphene/ACF hybrid, contributing abundant carboxyl moieties that electrostatically engage with the amine functionalities of DA. This innovative design not only augments the electrode’s surface area and conductivity but also fosters selective DA binding, thereby intensifying its redox reaction kinetics. The ensuing biosensor exhibits an extensive linear detection range spanning from 0.3 to 8.0 μM for DA, accompanied by an ultra-sensitive limit of detection at 13 nM. Remarkably, its outstanding performance in real-world biological matrices, such as human serum and urine, underscores its immense potential for applications in clinical diagnostics and monitoring therapeutic interventions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), a pivotal catecholamine neurotransmitter, holds a central role in orchestrating a myriad of biological processes within the central nervous and cardiovascular systems [1,2]. Its presence is indispensable for maintaining normal physiological functions, and imbalances in DA levels have been intricately linked to a range of neurological disorders, including Huntington's disease, schizophrenia, and Parkinson's disease [3]. These associations underscore the critical importance of precise DA quantification for both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes [4]. To address this need, researchers have devised an array of innovative analytical techniques aimed at accurately measuring DA concentrations. These methodologies encompass a diverse range, from biosensors leveraging advanced materials and technologies [5], to fluorescence-based assays that harness the power of light-emitting molecules for sensitive and selective DA detection [6,7]. Chromatographic, colorimetric, and chemiluminescence techniques have also emerged as formidable tools in this field, each offering unique advantages for DA analysis [8–12]. Among these various techniques, electrochemical methods have gained prominent attention due to their unparalleled combination of simplicity, swiftness, and remarkable sensitivity. However, the detection of DA is not without its challenges, particularly the interference posed by ascorbic acid and uric acid, which can significantly compromise the accuracy and reliability of DA quantification in complex, real-world samples [13,14]. To overcome these obstacles, researchers have relentlessly pursued innovative strategies aimed at enhancing the selectivity and sensitivity of conventional working electrodes for DA detection. These efforts have led to the development of novel electrode materials, surface modifications, and electrochemical protocols that can effectively discriminate between DA and its interfering molecules. These advancements have not only improved the accuracy and reliability of DA quantification but have also expanded the potential applications of electrochemical methods in clinical and research settings.

Carbon fibers (CFs), renowned for their exquisite one-dimensional structure and an arsenal of exceptional properties that include unparalleled strength, an impressive modulus, remarkable fatigue resistance, and minimal coefficients of expansion and friction, have firmly established themselves as premier candidates for microelectrode systems [15]. Their integration into such systems not only enhances sensitivity but also accelerates response times and facilitates the analysis of minute sample volumes, thereby fueling groundbreaking advancements in the fields of electrochemistry and biochemistry. Despite their inherent advantages, the inherently inert nature of CF surfaces poses a significant challenge, necessitating modifications to augment their functional capabilities and unlock their full potential [16]. Consequently, modified CFs have found widespread application in the development of high-performance composites and functional materials, where they contribute to the achievement of superior performance characteristics [17,18]. One notable example of the transformative power of CF modifications is the innovative work of Lan et al., who ingeniously crafted a highly sensitive hydrogen peroxide detection method. At the heart of this method lies a CF microelectrode surface, exquisitely adorned with Pt-Pd bimetallic nanocorals. This unique configuration leverages the synergistic effects of the Pt and Pd metals, coupled with the exceptional properties of CFs, to achieve remarkable sensitivity and selectivity towards hydrogen peroxide detection [19]. This breakthrough underscores the immense potential of modified CFs in advancing the capabilities of microelectrode systems and broadening their application horizons.

Graphene, with its exceptional electrical conductivity, expansive surface area, and electrochemical stability, has become a crucial component in advanced sensors and biosensors [20–22]. Among its derivatives, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) stands out for its superior conductivity and responsiveness [23]. This was demonstrated by Soltani et al., who used graphene nanosheets and a ferrocene ligand to create a selective voltammetric sensor for folic acid detection amidst interferences [24]. This success has spurred research into other layered materials, including graphene derivatives and emerging two-dimensional (2D) materials, for developing more sensitive, selective, and durable sensors. Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs), a class of fluorescent, metal-free nanomaterials, have gained attention due to their unique properties, such as low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, and electrocatalytic activity [25–27]. Wang et al. capitalized on these merits by creating a hybrid platform combining CDs and rGO on a CF microelectrode for highly sensitive dopamine detection [28]. This approach leveraged the best of both worlds, enhancing sensor performance and selectivity, resulting in a sensitive and reliable electrochemical sensor.

In this groundbreaking study, we embarked on an ambitious endeavor to create a novel electrochemical platform for DA detection by ingeniously integrating three-dimensional graphene nanosheets with activated carbon fibers (ACF). This innovative design was achieved through a meticulous process involving the electrodeposition of graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets and CDs. During the electrodeposition stage, we utilized an ionic liquid (IL) electrolyte, where the unique properties of the IL molecules played a pivotal role. Specifically, the imidazolium ring of [BMIM][BF4] interacted with the π-surface of graphene through a cation–π interaction, effectively preventing the restacking of graphene nanosheets and fostering the formation of a robust, three-dimensional graphene network [29]. This network not only provided a vast surface area but also facilitated efficient electron transfer, laying the foundation for a high-performance electrochemical sensor. Subsequently, CDs were elegantly electrodeposited onto the IL-modified rGO/ACF composite, introducing an abundance of carboxyl groups onto the surface. These carboxyl groups served as active sites, interacting with the amine functional groups of DA through electrostatic forces. This strategic interaction significantly enhanced the specificity of the sensor towards DA, enabling it to accurately detect even trace amounts of the neurotransmitter amidst complex biological matrices. Our innovative approach underscores the immense potential of graphene and CDs in constructing high-performance electrochemical biosensors. By harnessing the unique properties of these nanomaterials, we have successfully developed a platform that combines exceptional sensitivity, selectivity, and stability, paving the way for new advancements in biomedical diagnostics and research.

CF was procured from Fuel Cell Earth, USA. Graphite powder (average size of 600 nm) was obtained from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. Co. Ltd. Dopamine was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. [BMIM][BF4], sodium citrate, ammonium bicarbonate, sodium nitrate, sulfuric acid, potassium permanganate, hydrogen peroxide, L-ascorbic acid, uric acid, potassium phosphate dibasic, and potassium phosphate monobasic were sourced from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Factory (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without any additional purification.

All electrochemical experiments were conducted using a CHI-760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China). The reference electrode used was an Ag/AgCl electrode, and a platinum wire served as the counter electrode. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Thermo Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were captured with an S-3400 N microscope from Hitachi, Japan. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). pH measurements were carried out using a PB-10 basic pH meter (Sartorius Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd., China).

GO was synthesized utilizing the Hummer's method as a basis for the oxidation of graphite powder [30]. Briefly, a mixture of 2.0 g of graphite, 1.0 g of NaNO3, and 46.0 mL of H2SO4 was vigorously stirred in an ice bath to ensure thorough mixing. Afterward, 6.0 g of KMnO4 was cautiously added to the mixture in a controlled manner. Subsequently, the entire solution was transferred to a water bath, which was maintained at a temperature of 35 °C ± 5 °C, and stirred continuously for 2 h to yield a thick, viscous paste. Next, 92 mL of ultrapure water were introduced into the paste and the mixture was stirred for an additional 30 min at an elevated temperature of 90 °C ± 5 °C. Following this step, 280 mL of ultrapure water were added, and 4 mL of 30 % H2O2 were slowly dripped into the solution. The color transition from a dark brown to a yellow hue signified the successful completion of the oxidation reaction. The warm reaction mixture was then filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane to separate the solid product from the liquid. The resulting solid was thoroughly washed in succession with 200 mL of ultrapure water, 5 % HCl solution, and distilled water, each step repeated three times to ensure complete removal of impurities. Finally, the purified GO product was left to dry overnight in a vacuum oven at room temperature.

CDs were synthesized employing a previously reported methodology [31]. Essentially, 0.2 g of sodium citrate and 1.5 g of ammonium hydrogen carbonate were thoroughly dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, forming a homogeneous solution. This solution was then carefully sealed within a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave for the synthesis process. Following heating at a temperature of 180 °C for 4 h and subsequent cooling down to room temperature, the resulting mixture underwent purification via dialysis using a membrane with a specified molecular weight cutoff of 1 kDa for a duration of 12 h. After completion of the dialysis, the purified solution was carefully collected and stored at 4 °C for future applications and experiments.

The CFs underwent a meticulous cleaning process, involving repeated ultrasonic agitation in a sequential solvent bath of acetone, alcohol, and ultrapure water, to ensure a comprehensive removal of all impurities. Following this, the CFs were electrochemically activated in an equal-volume mixture of crude HNO3 and H2SO4 acids, with a constant voltage of 3 V applied against a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) for 10 min. To eliminate any remaining acidic residues, the activated CFs, now known as ACFs, were thoroughly rinsed with ultrapure water [32].

To prepare the GO suspension, GO was uniformly blended with the ionic liquid [BMIM][BF4], resulting in a homogeneous IL-GO mixture. This mixture was then subjected to vacuum drying at a gentle temperature of 30 °C for 48 h to remove any residual water molecules. The electrodeposition of GO nanosheets onto the ACFs was achieved within a standard three-electrode configuration, using the prepared IL-GO suspension at a concentration of 8 mg·mL−1. The deposition process was conducted under a stable voltage of −2.0 V versus an Ag/AgCl electrode for 6 min, with the ACF serving as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and a silver chloride electrode as the reference. This resulted in the formation of IL-reduced graphene oxide (IL-rGO) layers on the ACF substrate.

Additionally, the deposition of CDs onto the IL-rGO/ACF surface was achieved in an aqueous solution of 0.1 M Na2SO4, containing CDs at a concentration of 4 mg·mL−1. This was accomplished by maintaining a constant potential of −2.0 V against an Ag/AgCl electrode for 8 min at room temperature without stirring. The final composite material, referred to as CDs/IL-rGO/ACF, was thus obtained. A parallel synthesis of CDs/ACF was also carried out using the same protocol, enabling a systematic comparison and analysis of the modified electrochemical biosensors.

Within our laboratory's tightly controlled environment, serum and urine samples from a cohort of individuals exhibiting optimal health were meticulously handled. These human specimens underwent centrifugation at 10,000g for 5 min, efficiently segregating soluble components from cellular remnants. This step was crucial for obtaining clear and homogeneous samples for further analysis. To further refine the samples, a filtration process using a 0.22 μm membrane was implemented, guaranteeing their clarity and analytical suitability. This step was essential for ensuring the accuracy and reliability of our biosensor readings.

The refined samples were then carefully preserved at 4 °C, a temperature optimized for preserving the integrity of biomolecules. Prior to quantification, the samples were methodically spiked with DA solutions of varying concentrations, adhering strictly to the prescribed protocol. This step aimed to mimic various oxidative stress levels, pivotal for evaluating the redox homeostasis of the individuals. After thorough mixing, the samples underwent a precise 50-fold dilution with a 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) buffer adjusted to a pH of 6.0, enabling accurate measurements within the detection capabilities of our analytical system.

To ascertain the DA concentration in the samples, we utilized the reliable standard addition recovery method. This involved the addition of known quantities of DA to the samples, followed by the measurement of the total concentration. By comparing the measured values against the added standards, we accurately determined the initial DA content in the samples, offering crucial insights into the oxidative stress profiles of our study participants.

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) experiments were performed in a 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 6.0). The 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 6.0) was prepared by mixing appropriate amounts of potassium phosphate monobasic (KH2PO4) and potassium phosphate dibasic (K2HPO4). Specifically, the solution was prepared by dissolving 0.1 M KH2PO4 and 0.1 M K2HPO4 in ultrapure water, followed by adjusting the pH to 6.0 using a pH meter. This buffer system provides a stable pH environment for electrochemical measurements, ensuring consistent and reproducible results. The experimental setup was configured with the following parameters: an initial potential of −0.10 V, a final potential of 0.60 V, an amplitude of 0.05 V, a pulse width of 0.05 s, a sample width of 0.0167 s, a pulse period of 0.5 s, and a quiet time of 2.0 s. For the detection of DA, the DPV response was recorded at a relatively low working potential of 0.21 V, referenced against an Ag/AgCl electrode. All these measurements were conducted at ambient room temperature to ensure consistency and reproducibility. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of different electrodes were recorded in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at room temperature.

To gain a deeper understanding of the structural intricacies that contribute to the functionality of our electrochemical biosensor, we conducted extensive SEM studies on CFs that underwent various modifications to enhance their surface properties, conductivity, and overall performance. Fig. 1 A reveals a CF with a smooth surface and a diameter of 10 μm, which provides mechanical stability and facilitates uniform electrodeposition of active materials. This smooth texture, along with the CF's flexibility and conductivity, makes it an ideal structural backbone for the microelectrode, allowing it to adapt to different surfaces and maintain stable electrochemical communication. Additionally, the SEM studies enabled us to observe the modifications' impact on the CF's surface at a nanoscale level, providing insights into how changes in surface roughness, porosity, and nanoscale features affect the biosensor's electrochemical behavior. For example, the introduction of nanoporous structures or functional groups can enhance the surface area and promote electron transfer, resulting in improved sensitivity and response time. The electrochemical activation method we employed for the fabrication of ACF was instrumental in achieving a rougher surface texture, as observed in the SEM image shown in Fig. 1B. This enhanced surface area creates a more favorable environment for the deposition of GO nanosheets and CDs layers, ensuring strong adhesion and improved performance. As illustrated in Fig. 1C, the electrodeposition of GO nanosheets onto the ACF surface results in a dense, three-dimensional graphene layer. This transformation occurs due to the electrochemical reduction of GO at the ACF's surface under controlled conditions. The reduction process removes oxygen-containing functional groups, altering the hydrophilicity of the GO nanosheets. Simultaneously, π–π stacking interactions and minimal electrostatic repulsion help prevent re-aggregation of the graphene nanosheets, allowing them to maintain their individuality and form a tightly interconnected three-dimensional network. This network not only increases the surface area for electrochemical reactions but also promotes efficient electron transfer, making it an excellent platform for developing high-performance electrochemical biosensors.

After successfully depositing GO nanosheets onto the ACF surface, we further enhanced the modification by adding CDs, as shown in Fig. 1D. This step resulted in a uniform and dense coating of spherical CD particles on the ACF surface, contributing to the surface complexity and overall electrochemical properties of the composite material. Fig. 1E illustrates the remarkable enhancement in the anchoring of CDs onto the graphene nanosheets, facilitated by the large specific surface area of the three-dimensional graphene nanosheet layer. The intimate integration of CDs with the graphene nanosheets forms a tightly interconnected network that further increases the surface area and promotes efficient electron transfer. These findings confirm the effectiveness of our modification strategy and highlight the potential of the structured three-dimensional graphene nanosheet layer to enhance the performance of electrochemical biosensors. The synergistic combination of ACF, GO, and CDs offers a robust substrate, conductive bridge, and enhanced electrochemical activity, respectively, forming a highly functional and efficient platform poised to revolutionize biosensing with unprecedented sensitivity and selectivity.

To delve deeper into the intricate chemical makeup of the CDs/IL-rGO nanocomposite, we conducted a comprehensive XPS analysis, which offers a powerful tool for elucidating the elemental composition and chemical bonding states of materials at the atomic level. Fig. 2 A meticulously showcases the XPS spectra of CDs, GO, and CDs/IL-rGO/ACF, providing a clear picture of the elemental landscape of the nanocomposite. The spectra reveal that CDs/IL-rGO/ACF is primarily composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sodium, with distinct binding energy peaks observed at 284.79 eV (C 1s), 401.51 eV (N 1s), 530.99 eV (O 1s), and 1071.32 eV (Na 1s). These peaks underscore the complexity and diversity of the material's elemental constituents, each contributing to its unique properties and functionalities. To gain a more nuanced understanding of the carbon bonding configurations within the nanocomposite, we performed a detailed analysis of the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra. Fig. 2B presents the C1s curve fitting for CDs, revealing four distinct peaks that correspond to different carbon bonding states [31,33]. The peak at 284.79 eV is attributed to C=C bonds, indicative of the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms present in the aromatic rings of CDs. The peaks at 286.71 eV and 288.12 eV are assigned to C–O and C=O bonds, respectively, suggesting the presence of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of CDs. Lastly, the peak at 288.94 eV corresponds to O–C=O bonds, further confirming the existence of carboxylic acid groups. This analysis underscores the rich chemistry of CDs, with various carbon bonding configurations contributing to their unique properties.

In contrast, the C 1s XPS spectrum of GO in Fig. 2C exhibits a slightly different carbon bond distribution. Three peaks are observed, attributed to C–C (282.74 eV), C=C (284.80 eV), and C=O (285.57 eV) bonds [34,35]. The presence of C–C and C=C peaks indicates the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the graphene lattice, while the C=O peak suggests the oxidation of graphene during the GO synthesis process. The slight shift in the binding energies compared to CDs reflects the different chemical environments and bonding configurations in GO. Upon the successful electrodeposition of GO and CDs onto the surface of ACF, the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of the resultant CDs/IL-rGO/ACF nanocomposite, depicted in Fig. 2D, exhibits a fascinating blend of carbon bonding signatures. Specifically, four distinct peaks are identified, corresponding to C=C bonds at 284.81 eV, C–O bonds at 285.76 eV, C=O bonds at 286.65 eV, and O–C=O bonds at 288.83 eV. These peaks mirror the characteristic carbon bonding configurations observed in both CDs and GO, serving as a testament to the successful incorporation of these nanoscale building blocks onto the carbon fiber substrate [36]. The presence of all four carbon bonding states in the CDs/IL-rGO/ACF spectrum underscores the rich chemistry and potential for functionalization within the nanocomposite. The C=C bonds contribute to the structural integrity and electrical conductivity, while the C–O, C=O, and O–C=O bonds offer opportunities for further modification and interaction with other molecules or ions. This versatility makes CDs/IL-rGO/ACF an attractive candidate for a wide range of applications, including energy storage, catalysis, and environmental remediation, where the tailored properties of the nanocomposite can be harnessed to achieve desired outcomes.

To reinforce the insights gained from XPS analysis, FT-IR spectroscopy was employed as a complementary tool to delve deeper into the functional group landscape of CDs. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs, illustrated in Fig. 2E, unveils a series of characteristic absorption bands that serve as fingerprints of their chemical composition. Notably, the broad absorption band centered at 3400 cm−1 corresponds to O–H stretching vibrations, indicative of hydroxyl groups present on the CDs' surface. This is complemented by the sharp band at 1586 cm−1, attributed to C–C stretching vibrations, highlighting the aromatic nature of the carbon framework. Furthermore, the absorption band located at 1449 cm−1 signifies the presence of O–C=O groups, indicative of carbonyl functionalities, which can play a pivotal role in enhancing the electrochemical properties of the CDs. The band at 1397 cm−1 is associated with N–O stretching vibrations, suggesting the potential presence of nitro or nitroso groups, although this interpretation requires further confirmation based on the specific synthesis conditions. Lastly, the band at 1200 cm−1 is attributed to C–O–C vibrations, confirming the existence of ether linkages or ester functionalities within the CDs' structure [37,38]. The convergence of these FT-IR findings with the XPS results underscores the rich chemistry of CDs and their ability to participate in a multitude of chemical interactions. In particular, the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the CDs' surface facilitates electrostatic interactions with amine groups in dopamine, a key feature that enhances the specificity and sensitivity of the CDs/IL-rGO/ACF composite towards dopamine recognition. This tailored functionality, coupled with the amplified redox response, positions the composite as a promising candidate for advanced biosensing applications, where precise and rapid detection of biomolecules is crucial.

CV emerges as a pivotal tool in the analysis of surface properties and electrochemical responsiveness of modified electrodes. The examination of CV profiles for ACF, CDs/ACF, IL-rGO/ACF, and CDs/IL-rGO/ACF electrodes, conducted in a 0.1 M KCl solution fortified with 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, yields valuable insights. As vividly portrayed in Fig. 3 A, each sample's CV curve boasts a pronounced redox peak pair, mirroring the characteristic quasi-reversible, single-electron transfer dynamics of iron ions occurring at the microelectrode surfaces. The baseline ACF electrodes (depicted as curve a in Fig. 3A) exhibit a moderate electrochemical performance, marked by relatively subdued peak currents due to their inherently slower electron transfer kinetics. However, upon decorating the ACF surface with CDs (curve b in Fig. 3A), a notable surge in both anodic and cathodic peak currents is observed, attributable to CDs' exceptional conductivity. This initial enhancement underscores the potential of CDs as a conductivity-boosting modifier. Pushing the boundaries further, the introduction of IL-rGO onto the ACF via electrodeposition (curve c in Fig. 3A) transforms the electrode surface into a sophisticated three-dimensional nanostructure composed of rGO nanosheets. This architecture acts as a conduit for efficient electron transport, serving dual roles: enhancing conductivity akin to a nano-electrode array and establishing favorable pathways for electron flow, resulting in substantial augmentation of peak currents. The most remarkable transformation occurs when CDs and IL-rGO are synergistically combined on ACF (curve d in Fig. 3A). The resultant CDs/IL-rGO/ACF electrodes exhibit peak currents significantly exceeding those of their individually modified counterparts. This profound improvement stems from the multifaceted benefits conferred by the three-dimensional rGO layer. Not only does it enhance conductivity, but it also magnifies the electrode's specific surface area, providing ample real estate for CDs deposition. Moreover, the interplay between CDs and IL-rGO fosters a potent synergy that greatly accelerates electron transfer, culminating in an unprecedented elevation of the electrodes' electrochemical prowess. Collectively, these findings underscore the profound impact of strategic surface modifications on electrodes. By meticulously tailoring their compositions and structures, researchers can markedly amplify their electrochemical activity and performance, thereby unlocking new avenues for the advancement of high-performance electrochemical sensors with unparalleled sensitivity and efficiency.

The electrochemically active surface areas (ECSAs) of various electrodes were quantified to highlight the distinctions between these microelectrodes. This quantification utilized the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox couple as an electrochemical probe. The ECSA measurements for the electrodes were derived from the slope of the I p versus v 0.5 plot, adhering to the Randles-Sevcik equation [39]. The calculated ECSAs are as follows: 0.20 cm2 for ACF, 0.28 cm2 for CDs/ACF, 0.36 cm2 for IL-rGO/ACF, and 0.40 cm2 for CDs/IL-rGO/ACF, showcasing a progressive increase in surface area with the incorporation of carbon dots and ionic liquid-reduced graphene oxide.

To compare the electrochemical performance of electrodes modified with different materials, the standard electrochemical rate constant (k θ) is calculated using the method reported by Nicholson [40]. This method is applicable to quasi-reversible systems [41]. In the quasi-reversible reaction process, the rate of electron transfer is comparable to the rate of diffusion, and the peak potential separation (ΔE p) increases with the scan rate (v). Therefore, the k θ value can be determined according to Nicholson's method.

Where ψ is a kinetic parameter determined by ΔE p, and α is the charge transfer coefficient. D O and D R are the diffusion coefficients of the oxidized state of K3Fe(CN)6 and the reduced state of K4Fe(CN)6, respectively. F is the Faraday constant, and R and T have their usual meanings. The values of D O for K3Fe(CN)6 and D R for K4Fe(CN)6 are 7.63 × 10−6 and 6.32 × 10−6 cm2·s−1, respectively [42]. In most cases, the kinetics of reduction and oxidation are relatively symmetrical, and the value of α is set to 0.5 [43]. Therefore, the k θ value depends solely on the value of ψ (for a single electron process). According to the above equations, ψ shows a linear relationship with the inverse square root of the scan rate v −0.5, and its slope equals the value of k θ. As illustrated in Fig. 3B to 3E, the calculated k θ values for ACF, CDs/ACF, IL-rGO/ACF, and CDs/IL-rGO/ACF are 2.9 × 10−3 cm·s−1, 3.2 × 10−3 cm·s−1, 4.1 × 10−3 cm·s−1, and 6.4 × 10−3 cm·s−1, respectively. Therefore, the standard electrochemical rate constant of CDs/IL-rGO/ACF is higher than that of CDs/ACF and IL-rGO/ACF, indicating a faster electron transfer rate for ACF modified with both CDs and IL-rGO.

The exceptional performance of CDs/IL-rGO/ACF is further exemplified through its CV response towards 5 μM DA in a 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 6.0) environment. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the absence of discernible redox peaks in ACF and CDs/ACF suggests their limited electrochemical activity in this specific context. In stark contrast, IL-rGO/ACF and CDs/IL-rGO/ACF display pronounced redox peaks centered at approximately 0.21 V, signifying the effective reduction of DA at these modified electrodes. Notably, CDs/IL-rGO/ACF exhibits significantly higher anodic and cathodic peak currents compared to the other electrodes, emphasizing the potent synergy between CDs and IL-rGO. This underscores the promising electrochemical capabilities of CDs/IL-rGO/ACF, positioning it as a prime candidate for advanced sensor applications.

To delve deeper into the electrochemical behavior of 5 μM DA at the CDs/IL-rGO/ACF interface, we varied the scan rate and observed the subsequent changes. Fig. 4B illustrates the CV curves recorded for 5 μM DA at the CDs/IL-rGO/ACF surface, immersed in 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 6.0), as the scan rate escalates from 10 mV·s−1 to 200 mV·s−1. Notably, as the scan rate accelerates, both the anodic and cathodic peak currents of DA at the CDs/IL-rGO/ACF surface undergo a concurrent enhancement. Fig. 4C presents a compelling picture, where both the oxidation peak current (I pa) and reduction peak current (I pc) maintain strong linear correlations with the scan rate, spanning the range from 10 mV·s−1 to 200 mV·s−1. Specifically, the linear regression equations are derived as I pc (μA) = − 0.06623 − 26.70v (V·s−1) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9989, and I pa (μA) = − 0.3355 + 23.92v (V·s−1) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9969. These findings unequivocally indicate that the oxidation process of DA on the CDs/IL-rGO/ACF surface is predominantly surface-controlled, as opposed to being diffusion-limited [44], underscoring the efficient electron transfer kinetics at this interface.

The exceptional performance of CDs/IL-rGO/ACF is vividly demonstrated through its DPV response towards 5 μM DA in a 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 6.0). As depicted in Fig. 4D, the DPV peak current achieved by CDs/IL-rGO/ACF (curve d) stands out prominently, surpassing that of ACF (curve a), CDs/ACF (curve b), and IL-rGO/ACF (curve c). Specifically, the DPV peak currents for 5 μM DA at these four microelectrodes are: 1.09 μA for ACF, 2.45 μA for CDs/ACF, 6.81 μA for IL-rGO/ACF, and an impressive 12.94 μA for CDs/IL-rGO/ACF (inset in Fig. 4D). This remarkable enhancement can be attributed to the synergistic effects of the three-dimensional graphene nanosheets, which boast high conductivity and a vast specific surface area. This expansive surface area not only provides ample room for CDs deposition but also facilitates efficient electron transfer. Additionally, the negatively charged carboxyl groups adorning the CDs surface engage in electrostatic interactions with the amine groups present in DA, thereby significantly accelerating the redox reaction kinetics of DA. Collectively, these findings underscore the exceptional potential of the CDs/IL-rGO nanocomposite as an outstanding electrode material, poised to revolutionize the construction of highly sensitive and efficient DA electrochemical biosensors.

To enhance the responsiveness and precision of CDs/IL-rGO/ACF for DA detection, a meticulous optimization process was undertaken, encompassing a wide array of experimental parameters. Specifically, the optimization involved meticulously tuning the concentration of IL-rGO, fine-adjusting its deposition potential and duration, as well as exploring the optimal concentration, deposition potential, and deposition time of CDs. Additionally, the pH level of the 0.1 M PBS buffer was also optimized to create an ideal environment for the sensing reaction.

Fig. 5A illustrates the profound impact of IL-rGO concentration on the DPV peak current. As the IL-rGO concentration escalates from 2.0 mg·mL−1 to 8.0 mg·mL−1, a distinct increase in the DPV peak current is observed, indicative of improved DA detection sensitivity. However, beyond 8.0 mg·mL−1, the peak current remains largely stable, even as the IL-rGO concentration rises to 10.0 mg·mL−1. This plateau suggests that the electrodeposition of IL-rGO on the ACF surface reaches saturation at a concentration of 8.0 mg·mL−1, thereby confirming the optimal IL-rGO concentration for maximizing DA detection efficiency. Furthermore, a detailed investigation into the electrodeposition conditions of IL-rGO was conducted, with a focus on both deposition potential and time. As depicted in Fig. 5B and C, a systematic exploration led to the identification of optimal deposition conditions for IL-rGO/ACF construction. Specifically, a deposition potential of −2.0 V and a deposition time of 6 min were found to be the ideal parameters, as they resulted in the most favorable electrode surface morphology and performance for DA detection. These optimized conditions ensure a uniform and efficient deposition of IL-rGO onto the ACF, thereby maximizing the sensor's sensitivity and reproducibility.

Fig. 5D showcases the remarkable influence of CDs concentration on the DPV peak current. With a gradual increase in CDs concentration from 1.0 mg·mL−1 to 4.0 mg·mL−1, a pronounced enhancement in the DPV peak current is evident, underscoring an improvement in the sensitivity of DA detection. However, once the CDs concentration surpasses 4.0 mg·mL−1, further increments up to 5.0 mg·mL−1 yield minimal to no change in the peak current, indicating that the electrodeposition of CDs onto the IL-rGO/ACF surface has reached saturation at a concentration of 4.0 mg·mL−1. This finding validates 4.0 mg·mL−1 as the optimal CDs concentration for optimizing DA detection efficiency. Moreover, a comprehensive study was undertaken to delve into the optimal electrodeposition conditions for CDs. This analysis encompassed a meticulous examination of both the deposition potential and time. As presented in Fig. 5E and 5F, a rigorous exploration process revealed the ideal deposition parameters for CDs/IL-rGO/ACF fabrication. Notably, a deposition potential of −2.0 V and a deposition duration of 6 min were determined to be the most suitable, as they facilitated the creation of an electrode surface that exhibited optimal morphology and performance for DA detection. These optimized conditions facilitate a uniform and efficient deposition of CDs onto the IL-rGO/ACF, ultimately enhancing the sensor's sensitivity and reproducibility.

Concurrently, the influence of pH on the electrochemical properties of DA was examined in a 0.1 M PBS buffer solution. As depicted in Fig. 5G, a pH of 6.0 in the PBS buffer was found to be the optimal condition for DA detection. Fig. 5 H further reveals that as the pH was varied within the range of 5.0 to 9.0, a notable negative shift in the oxidation–reduction peak potentials of DA was observed. Notably, the oxidation peak potentials of DA exhibited a linear correlation with pH, following the equation E pa (V) = − 0.06378 pH + 0.6079, with a high correlation coefficient of 0.9914. These findings imply that when the slope approximates −0.059 V/pH, the CDs/IL-rGO/ACF system adheres to the Nernst equation, indicative of the involvement of two protons and two electrons in the electrochemical oxidation of DA [45].

Under meticulously crafted and refined experimental conditions, an exhaustive and meticulous investigation was undertaken to delve deeply into the intricate detection capabilities of the innovative electrochemical biosensor specifically designed for DA detection. The precision and rigor of these conditions ensured that the biosensor's performance was evaluated under optimal parameters, maximizing its potential for sensitivity and accuracy. As the graphical representation in Fig. 6 A vividly illustrates, the DPV current response displayed a consistent and significant enhancement as the concentration of DA gradually increased. This steadfast augmentation underscores the biosensor's exquisite sensitivity to even subtle fluctuations in DA levels, demonstrating its robustness and reliability in detecting minute changes. Moreover, the inset of Fig. 6B presents a compelling and robust linear correlation that spans a wide range of DA concentrations, from 0.3 to 8.0 µM. This correlation is succinctly characterized by the linear regression equation ΔI (μA) = − 3.101[DA] (µM) + 0.1633, which is accompanied by an impressive correlation coefficient of 0.9961, indicative of a strong and dependable linear relationship. The high correlation coefficient further underscores the biosensor's precision and consistency in detecting DA concentrations within this range. The remarkable linearity of this relationship directly translates into an exceptionally low detection limit of 0.013 µM, a testament to the biosensor's unparalleled sensitivity. This detection limit was meticulously calculated using a rigorous method that involves determining the ratio of three times the standard deviation obtained from 11 blank samples to the slope of the linear regression equation. This meticulous approach ensures that the detection limit is both reliable and reproducible, highlighting the biosensor's potential for detecting even the smallest traces of DA.

A comparative analysis presented in Table 1 reveals that the three-dimensional graphene-based DA electrochemical biosensor, when juxtaposed against its predecessors, stands out with unparalleled sensitivity and a reduced detection limit. The exceptional structural attributes of three-dimensional graphene, such as its exceptional surface-to-volume ratio, unparalleled conductivity, and impeccable biocompatibility, conspire to endow the sensing interface with a myriad of active sites. These attributes facilitate seamless electron transfer and efficient biomolecular recognition mechanisms, thereby enhancing the overall performance of the biosensor. This groundbreaking design not only broadens the horizons of electrochemical biosensor architectures but also opens up new avenues for the early detection and continuous monitoring of dopamine-related disorders. By harnessing the unique properties of three-dimensional graphene, this innovative biosensor offers promising solutions for advancing healthcare diagnostics and treatments, particularly in the realm of neurological conditions where dopamine imbalances play a pivotal role.

Our biosensor, utilizing IL-rGO on ACF substrate with CDs electrodeposition, demonstrates several notable advantages. The IL-rGO/ACF composite provides a large specific surface area and enhanced conductivity, which contribute to the biosensor's high sensitivity and rapid electron transfer. The CDs electrodeposition further enhances the selectivity towards DA through specific chemical interactions with its amine functional groups. This approach offers a simple and cost-effective fabrication process, making it suitable for large-scale production. However, it is important to recognize that each sensor technology has its unique strengths and limitations. For instance, some sensors based on metal oxide nanomaterials may exhibit high selectivity but require complex fabrication processes and high temperatures, which can increase costs and environmental impact. On the other hand, sensors utilizing conducting polymers may offer good sensitivity and stability but may suffer from limited reproducibility and scalability. Our biosensor stands out due to its combination of high sensitivity, selectivity, cost-effectiveness, and scalability. However, there is always room for improvement, and future research could explore further optimization of the IL-rGO/ACF/CDs composite, as well as the integration of additional functional materials to enhance the biosensor's performance even further. In summary, this innovative electrochemical biosensor, with its unparalleled sensitivity and low detection limit, showcases its immense potential for highly sensitive and accurate analytical applications. Its ability to detect minute, trace amounts of DA opens up new avenues for research and development in various fields, including neuroscience, pharmacology, and clinical diagnostics, where precise and reliable DA detection is crucial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, to comprehensively evaluate the practical application potential of the fabricated electrochemical biosensor, we delved into its selectivity amidst the influence of coexisting substances in complex environments. The biosensor's selectivity was rigorously evaluated amidst potential interfering substances commonly encountered in real-world detection scenarios. As depicted in Fig. 7 , rigorous challenge tests were designed, incorporating high concentrations of common ions (such as 300-fold Na+, K+, Cl−, NO3 −) and divalent ions (including 200-fold Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, SO4 2−), alongside a range of biomolecules (such as 150-fold glucose, lysine, cysteine, citric acid, glutamic acid, tryptophan, urea, and ascorbic acid), and even higher concentrations (10-fold) of urea and ascorbic acid, all of which are potential interferences encountered in real-world detection scenarios. Under these conditions, the detection of 1 µM DA revealed a remarkable current signal loss contained within ±5 %, underscoring the exceptional selectivity of the CDs/IL-rGO/ACF electrochemical biosensor. This outstanding performance is attributable not only to the stable platform and high conductivity provided by the IL-rGO/ACF substrate but also to the unique benefits derived from the further electrodeposition of CDs onto IL-rGO/ACF.

The introduction of IL-rGO onto the ACF substrate transformed the electrode surface into a sophisticated three-dimensional nanostructure, enhancing conductivity and providing a large specific surface area for efficient electron transfer. This enhancement provides more binding sites for ions, thereby improving the capture efficiency for target ions like dopamine. Further electrodeposition of CDs onto IL-rGO/ACF enriched the surface properties of the electrode, such as wettability and charge distribution, which aids in selectively attracting or repelling specific types of ions. Notably, the carboxyl functional groups on the surface of CDs engage in electrostatic interactions with the amine groups of DA molecules, enhancing selective recognition towards dopamine. Furthermore, the specific chemical groups within the modification layer participate in chemical interactions with target ions, forming stable complexes or ion pairs. This creates a unique chemical environment conducive to distinguishing and extracting target ions from complex samples. By optimizing the composition and structure of the modification layer, interference from other coexisting ions is effectively minimized. For instance, the IL component within the modification layer may further enhance dopamine recognition through its unique ion conductivity and selectivity, while inhibiting responses from other ions. Lastly, the graphene component within the modification layer exhibits excellent conductivity, facilitating rapid electron transfer between the electrode and target ions. This efficient electron transfer enhances the sensor's sensitivity and response speed, enabling more accurate identification of target ions amidst multiple ion coexistence. This mechanism effectively excluded interference from other coexisting substances, enhancing the biosensor's specificity towards DA detection.

To ensure the accuracy of detection, a rigorous examination of the reproducibility and stability of the CDs/IL-rGO/ACF electrochemical biosensor was conducted. Across five consecutive tests using the same modified electrode, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the response towards 1 µM DA was found to be merely 1.4 % (Fig. 8 A). This minimal deviation underscores the high consistency of the sensor during repetitive operations, highlighting its exceptional reproducibility and reliability of the detection results. Furthermore, to validate the sensor's long-term stability in practical applications, it was subjected to a storage test spanning 15 days. Encouragingly, even after this extended period, the sensor's response towards 1 µM DA remained stable, with an RSD of only 6.3 % (Fig. 8B). This outcome underscores the CDs/IL-rGO/ACF electrochemical biosensor's remarkable ability to maintain stable detection performance over time. In future, we will explore more stable materials and surface coatings as potential solutions to enhance the biosensor's durability and robustness in complex environments.

After thoroughly examining and validating the performance metrics of our electrochemical biosensor, we ventured further by applying it to the detection of DA in real biological samples–human serum and urine. This step not only posed a significant challenge to the sensor's capabilities but also marked a solid progression towards its clinical application. To ensure the rigor and representativeness of our experiments, we meticulously selected adult male and female volunteers from diverse health backgrounds as sample contributors. With their informed consent, invaluable serum and urine samples were obtained. Recognizing the diversity and complexity of biological samples as crucial factors in assessing sensor performance, we adhered to standardized protocols, diluting the samples 100-fold with 0.1 M PBS buffer (pH 6.0) to minimize matrix effects on detection outcomes. Subsequently, we precisely spiked three representative concentrations of DA (0.5, 1, and 3 µM) into the diluted samples, spanning a wide range from low to high, to comprehensively evaluate the sensor's responsiveness and accuracy across different concentration levels. Through a meticulously designed recovery test, we systematically recorded the sensor's detection results for DA in the samples and compared them against theoretical values.

As shown in Table 2 , the sensor exhibited exceptionally high recovery rates, ranging stably between 94.48 % and 106.5 %, in both human serum and urine samples. This outcome not only underscores the sensor's accuracy, stability, and reliability in detecting DA within complex biological matrices but also underscores its vast potential and applicability in various fields, including clinical diagnostics, drug monitoring, and biomedical research. In conclusion, our electrochemical biosensor has demonstrated outstanding performance in detecting DA in human serum and urine, with its high recovery rates, stability, and accuracy serving as a strong foundation for future advancements in these domains. We are confident that with continuous technological advancements and optimizations, this sensor will continue to showcase its unique value and charm across a broader spectrum of applications.

In summary, we ingeniously combined ionic liquid-assisted technology with electrodeposition reduction to decorate carbon fiber surfaces with a three-dimensional rGQ nanosheet architecture, thereby establishing a highly sensitive platform for DA detection. Furthermore, the innovative incorporation of CDs as co-modifiers imparted abundant carboxyl functional groups to the ACF surface, enabling strong electrostatic interactions with the amine groups in DA. This significantly enhanced the rate of DA's redox reaction. The resulting electrochemical biosensor exhibited a remarkable linear relationship within a broad concentration range of 0.3–8.0 μM DA, coupled with an ultra-low detection limit of 13 nM. Most notably, the sensor performed exceptionally well in detecting DA in real biological samples such as human serum and urine, offering immense potential for applications in disease treatment monitoring and clinical diagnostics.

The authors of this study affirm that they hold no conflicts of interest and have adhered to stringent ethical guidelines throughout the entire research endeavor. Prior to embarking on any experiments that entailed human participation, meticulous efforts were made to secure informed consent from all individuals involved, thereby ensuring that their autonomy and rights were upheld with the utmost respect.

The present research, which incorporated the analysis of human serum and urine samples, was executed in strict accordance with the Guidelines of China (WS/T 225-2002), a framework that meticulously outlines ethical considerations and practices in biomedical research. The study protocol underwent a rigorous review process by the Ethics Committee at Nanning Normal University, who granted their approval, signifying that the research adhered to the highest ethical standards and principles.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shan Huang received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Wuhan University in 2009. After a two-year postdoctoral (2009–2011) stay at the Institut Armand-Frappier, Institut National de la Recherche Scientifique (INRS) and INRS-Énergie, Matériaux et Télécommunications, Université du Québec, Canada, she returned to China as a professor at Nanning Normal University. Her current research interests are the efficient construction of multi-functional nano-optical/electrochemical sensors and their application in biomedical analysis. She has published more than 100 papers in international journals. From 2019 to 2023, she has been included in the “Top 2% of the world's top Scientists” list for five consecutive years.

Qi Xiao received his Ph.D. degree in physical chemistry from Wuhan University in 2010. After a one-year postdoctoral (2010–2011) stay at the University of Massachusetts, USA, he returned to China as a professor at Nanning Normal University. His current research interests are the synthesis of functional graphene nanomaterials and their application in electrochemical sensors. He has published more than 130 papers in international journals. From 2019 to 2023, he has been included in the “Top 2% of the world's top Scientists” list for five consecutive years.
